papers.title: Crystalline silica particles exposure modulated miRNA expression profiles in human macrophage-like THP-1 cells.
papers.abstract: Occupational and environmental exposure to crystalline silica particles is a major global health concern linked to silicosis, pulmonary fibrosis, autoimmune disease, and lung cancer. MicroRNAs (miRNAs) regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally and are emerging as potential effect biomarkers of exposure in human biomonitoring studies. To identify intracellular and extracellular miRNAs responses to silica-particle exposure in human THP-1 derived macrophages, differentiated THP-1 derived macrophages were exposed to 0-300 µg/mL crystalline silica particles for 24 h. Cytotoxicity was assessed
papers.summary: This study shows that exposure to crystalline silica particles alters the expression of microRNAs (miRNAs) in human macrophage-like cells, identifying both intracellular and extracellular miRNAs that may serve as biomarkers for silica exposure and its associated diseases.

papers.title: Molecular Safeguards of Survival: De novo transcriptome assembly and tissue-specific transcriptomic profiling of the yellow-foot clam Paphia malabarica.
papers.abstract: The yellow-foot clam, Paphia malabarica Chemnitz, 1782 is a regionally important fishery and aquaculture resource along the southwest coast of India. The fishery of this species from the Ashtamudi estuary has been certified as sustainable by the Marine Stewardship Council. We generated the first high-quality, comprehensive de novo transcriptome assembly of Paphia malabarica using Illumina paired-end sequencing. A total of 729.2 million raw reads were generated and further processed and assembled using the Trinity assembler. The final assembly yielded 55,980 predicted coding sequences (CDS) wit
papers.summary: This study presents the first high-quality de novo transcriptome assembly for the yellow-foot clam, an important fishery resource in India. Using Illumina sequencing, researchers generated over 729 million reads and assembled 55,980 coding sequences, providing a foundational genomic resource for understanding the biology and sustainability of this species.

papers.title: A single-cell transcriptomic atlas of the pigtail macaque placenta in late gestation.
papers.abstract: The placenta is a complex organ with multiple immune and non-immune cell types that promote fetal tolerance and facilitate the transfer of nutrients and oxygen. The nonhuman primate (NHP) is a key experimental model for studying human pregnancy complications, in part due to similarities in placental structure, which makes it essential to understand how single-cell populations compare across the human and NHP maternal-fetal interface. We constructed a single-cell RNA-Seq (scRNA-Seq) atlas of the placenta from the pigtail macaque (Macaca nemestrina) in the third trimester, comprising three diffe
papers.summary: This study presents a single-cell RNA sequencing atlas of the pigtail macaque placenta during late gestation, identifying diverse immune and non-immune cell types. By comparing these cell populations to those in humans, the work highlights similarities and differences at the maternal-fetal interface, providing a valuable resource for understanding pregnancy complications using nonhuman primate models.

papers.title: Multi-omics analysis of extracellular vesicle cargo in cancer.
papers.abstract: Extracellular vesicles (EVs) are increasingly recognized as minimally invasive sources of molecular information in cancer, carrying nucleic acids, proteins, lipids, and metabolites that reflect tumor biology. Recent advances in high-throughput sequencing and mass spectrometry have not only enabled multi-omics profiling of EV cargo but also revealed that different molecular layers capture distinct yet complementary aspects of cancer processes. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the rapidly expanding landscape of EV multi-omics, highlighting methodological constraints, the predomin
papers.summary: This review discusses how extracellular vesicles (EVs) from tumors carry diverse molecular cargo (nucleic acids, proteins, lipids, metabolites) that can be profiled using multi-omics approaches to reveal complementary aspects of cancer biology. It highlights recent technological advances in sequencing and mass spectrometry that enable such profiling, while also addressing methodological challenges in the field.

papers.title: MIND: multimodal integration with neighbourhood-aware distributions.
papers.abstract: Multimodal data integration combines different data modalities to improve predictive and classification performance. In biology, multi-omics profiling has become a powerful tool for applications such as cancer patient stratification. However, integration of multi-omics data remains challenging because of missingness and inherent heterogeneity. Methods such as imputation and sample exclusion often rely on strong assumptions that could lead to information loss or distortion. To address these limitations, we propose MIND (Multimodal Integration with Neighbourhood-aware Distributions), which learn
papers.summary: MIND is a new method for integrating multi-omics data that handles missing data and heterogeneity without relying on strong assumptions like imputation or sample exclusion. It learns neighborhood-aware distributions to combine different data modalities, improving cancer patient stratification and other biological analyses.

papers.title: A pragmatist approach to bridging tables and ontologies through LinkML and punning.
papers.abstract: Ontologies support data integration and knowledge discovery across multiple life science disciplines through the application of formal semantics that define hierarchical and cross-hierarchical relationships which enable automated reasoning capabilities. At the same time, tabular data is by far the most commonly produced format of data in most disciplines. This is especially true in biodiversity dataset production. Creating rich semantic data from tables can be complex because of a mismatch between flat tabular data and hierarchical ontology models. Here, we present a practical approach for clo
papers.summary: This paper presents a practical method using LinkML and punning to bridge the gap between flat tabular data and hierarchical ontology models, enabling richer semantic data integration. The approach simplifies the conversion of tabular biodiversity data into ontology-compliant formats without requiring deep ontological expertise.

papers.title: Vitiligo Information Resource database v3.
papers.abstract: Vitiligo is a multifaceted autoimmune skin disease characterized by the loss of epidermal melanocytes, yet its molecular basis remains poorly understood. To address the need for a specialized systems biology interface, we present VIRdb v3.0, an integrative database combining curated transcriptomics-based datasets with differentially expressed genes, a protein-protein interaction network highlighting regulatory hubs, and an enriched library of natural compounds and FDA-approved drugs targeting vitiligo-related proteins. The platform supports pathway enrichment, molecular docking, and cross-dise
papers.summary: VIRdb v3.0 is an updated database that integrates transcriptomics data, protein-protein interaction networks, and drug libraries to help researchers study the molecular basis of vitiligo, an autoimmune skin disease. It provides tools for pathway enrichment and molecular docking, making it a specialized resource for systems biology analysis of vitiligo.

papers.title: Investigation of potential targets and mechanisms of naringenin in the treatment of spinal cord injury: A network pharmacology, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulation study.
papers.abstract: Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a disease that causes significant functional impairment and high mortality, imposing a heavy economic burden on patients and society. In this in-silico study, we investigated the potential therapeutic targets and underlying mechanisms of naringenin (NAR) in SCI by integrating network pharmacology, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics (MD) simulation. The intersection of NAR and SCI targets was used to construct a protein-protein interaction network using the STRING database. Gene Ontology, Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes, and Wiki Pathway enrichment ana
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods (network pharmacology, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulations) to identify potential protein targets and pathways through which the natural compound naringenin might treat spinal cord injury. The findings suggest naringenin may act on multiple targets and signaling pathways, providing a theoretical basis for future experimental validation.

papers.title: Spatial omics illuminates tumor heterogeneity.
papers.abstract: Intratumoral heterogeneity (ITH) is the coexistence of diverse cancer cell states, genotypes, and microenvironmental niches within a single tumor and is a major driver of therapeutic resistance and disease progression. While the clinical implications of ITH are well appreciated, conventional methods and models have been limited in resolving the spatial and functional complexity underlying ITH. Recent advances in single-cell and spatial omics, high-plex imaging, and in vivo CRISPR perturbation and lineage tracing now enable unprecedented, mechanistic dissection of tumor ecosystems. These techno
papers.summary: This paper reviews how recent advances in spatial omics, single-cell technologies, and in vivo CRISPR tools are enabling researchers to dissect the complex spatial and functional heterogeneity within tumors, which is a major driver of treatment resistance and disease progression.

papers.title: Protocol for integrating and interpreting multi-omics data combining unsupervised and supervised data integrating approaches.
papers.abstract: Multi-omics integration combines data from transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics to provide insights into biological systems. Here, we present a protocol for integrating and interpreting multi-omics data using unsupervised multi-omics factor analysis (MOFA), supervised projection-based integration (data integration analysis for biomarker discovery using latent components, DIABLO), along with general single-omics analysis techniques and visualizations. We describe steps for data preparation, model construction, and biological interpretation of multi-omics datasets. These approaches iden
papers.summary: This protocol provides a step-by-step guide for integrating transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics data using both unsupervised (MOFA) and supervised (DIABLO) methods. It covers data preparation, model building, and biological interpretation, enabling researchers to identify biomarkers and understand complex biological systems.

papers.title: MetaHarmonizer: robust biomedical metadata harmonization and a contamination control for inflated LLM performance on public benchmarks.
papers.abstract: Public biomedical repositories hold substantial reuse potential, but inconsistent metadata routinely blocks integration across studies. Recent LLM-based harmonization approaches address scale but suffer from non-determinism, hallucinated ontology terms, and, in their highest-accuracy configurations, dependence on proprietary APIs or labeled fine-tuning data. A more fundamental concern is that LLM accuracies on widely-used public benchmarks may substantially inflate transferable capability: under a contamination-controlled evaluation protocol we developed, the apparent LLM-only advantage on the
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This paper introduces MetaHarmonizer, a tool for robust biomedical metadata harmonization that addresses key limitations of LLM-based approaches, including non-determinism, hallucinated ontology terms, and dependence on proprietary APIs. The authors also develop a contamination-controlled evaluation protocol revealing that LLM performance on public benchmarks may be substantially inflated, questioning the true transferable capability of these models.

papers.title: Cutibacterium acnes induce T cell extracellular traps with distinct proteome programs.
papers.abstract: Cutibacterium acnes (C. acnes) is a common skin commensal associated in acne pathogenesis. Here, we investigated whether acne-associated (HL043PA1, CA) and healthy skin-associated (HL110PA3, CH) C. acnes strains differentially induce extracellular traps (ET) in human neutrophils (NETs) and Th17 cells (TETs) and defined their proteomic and functional properties. Both strains triggered rapid ET release from neutrophils and Th17 cells. However, whereas NETs displayed a conserved proteomic profile regardless of strain, TETs exhibited strain-specific specialization. CA-induced TETs were enriched in
papers.summary: This study shows that different strains of the skin bacterium Cutibacterium acnes trigger distinct immune responses: while neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) have a consistent protein composition regardless of strain, T cell extracellular traps (TETs) show strain-specific proteomic profiles, with acne-associated strains inducing a specialized TET response.

papers.title: Next-Generation Bionic Sensors for Small Molecule Detection: Integrating Synthetic Biology, Nanomaterials, and Artificial Intelligence.
papers.abstract: Bionic sensors are emerging as powerful analytical platforms driving the development of next-generation detection technologies, particularly for small molecule sensing in complex environmental and biological systems. However, accurate and selective detection of small molecules remains fundamentally challenging due to their low molecular weight, limited structural specificity, and strong interference from complex matrices. This review provides a comprehensive overview of recent advances in bionic sensor technologies, focusing on how the integration of synthetic biology, nanomaterials, and artif
papers.summary: This review discusses recent advances in bionic sensors for detecting small molecules, which are challenging due to their small size and interference from complex samples. It highlights how combining synthetic biology, nanomaterials, and artificial intelligence can improve sensor accuracy and selectivity for environmental and biological applications.

papers.title: From parasite biology to predictive metabolic models.
papers.abstract: Protist parasites cause devastating diseases worldwide, yet their complex metabolism remains poorly understood. Genome-scale metabolic models (GEMs) have emerged as powerful tools to systematically represent and simulate parasite metabolism, enabling the prediction of gene essentiality, metabolic vulnerabilities, and host-parasite interactions. This review examines the current landscape of GEMs for protist pathogens, focusing on the key modeling decisions (objective functions, constraints, and compartmentalization) that govern model behavior and predictive scope. We discuss how these choices s
papers.summary: This review discusses how genome-scale metabolic models (GEMs) can systematically represent and simulate the metabolism of protist parasites, which cause devastating diseases but have poorly understood metabolism. The authors examine key modeling decisions such as objective functions, constraints, and compartmentalization that determine model behavior and predictive power for identifying gene essentiality and host-parasite interactions.

papers.title: Two Australian genome assemblies expand the genomic blueprint of giant kelp.
papers.abstract: Giant kelp, Macrocystis pyrifera (order Laminariales), occurs across the temperate coasts of the Northern and Southern Hemispheres and is at high risk from ocean warming. Few giant kelp forests remain across the Southeast Australian shelf, and some sites are currently under active restoration. Genomic resources can greatly aid in the conservation of remnant populations and enhance restoration efforts. Reference genomes are a fundamental resource as they are either prerequisites for, or substantially improve, many analyses used in conservation genomics. A single reference genome is available fo
papers.summary: This study presents two new reference genomes for giant kelp (Macrocystis pyrifera) from Australian populations, expanding the genomic resources available for this ecologically important species. These genomes will aid conservation and restoration efforts by enabling population genomics analyses to understand and preserve remaining kelp forests threatened by ocean warming.

papers.title: The first chromosome-scale genome assembly for Dermacentor reticulatus: a key vector of tick-borne pathogens of public and veterinary health importance in Europe.
papers.abstract: Dermacentor reticulatus is a key tick species across Europe and an established vector of multiple pathogens affecting both human and animal health. Despite its significant role in disease transmission and its expanding distribution, genomic data for this species remain limited. Here, we present the first chromosome-scale genome assembly of D. reticulatus, constructed using Oxford Nanopore long-read sequencing.
papers.summary: Researchers have produced the first chromosome-scale genome assembly for Dermacentor reticulatus, a tick species that spreads diseases affecting humans and animals across Europe. This new genomic resource will help scientists better understand tick biology and develop improved strategies for controlling tick-borne diseases.

papers.title: Intelligent english education resource recommendation via LLM-knowledge graph integration and ecological niche fitness modeling.
papers.abstract: Teaching English to large and varied student populations has made adaptive, learner-aware resource recommendation a pressing need, yet most deployed systems still lean on collaborative filtering or content heuristics that read learners only at the surface. Such systems tend to stumble in four recurring ways: they miss the finer grain of linguistic competence, they reason over sparse and brittle knowledge associations, they falter whenever a learner or a resource is new, and they rarely travel well to settings their training never saw. We address these problems together through a framework in w
papers.summary: This paper presents a framework that combines large language models with knowledge graphs and ecological niche fitness modeling to improve English education resource recommendations. The approach aims to overcome limitations of traditional recommendation systems by better capturing learners' linguistic competence, handling sparse data, and adapting to new learners and contexts.

papers.title: Multimodal temporal feature fusion for teacher competency assessment and precision training resource recommendation.
papers.abstract: Assessing teacher competency in a reliable and multidimensional manner remains an open problem, largely because conventional evaluation instruments capture only a fraction of the behavioral repertoire that defines effective instruction. We tackle this challenge by developing an integrated framework that fuses heterogeneous classroom signals-video, audio, transcribed text, and physiological recordings-through modality-specific encoders coupled with a cross-modal attention mechanism. The attention module adaptively re-weights each data stream according to its diagnostic relevance for a given com
papers.summary: This paper presents a multimodal AI framework that fuses video, audio, text, and physiological data from classrooms to assess teacher competency and recommend personalized training resources. The cross-modal attention mechanism adaptively weights each data stream based on its diagnostic relevance, improving assessment reliability over single-modality approaches.

papers.title: Multimodal genomic prediction is not a buzzword: why modern plant breeding must integrate genomics, enviromics, and phenomics.
papers.abstract: Plant breeding has entered a new era, transitioning from a predominantly unimodal, genomics-focused paradigm to one defined by multimodal data integration. Dense genomic markers, long-term environmental datasets, physiological and omics measurements, and high-throughput phenotyping platforms are now routine components of modern breeding programs. Paradoxically, while plant breeding now operates in a multimodal data ecosystem, most prediction models remain effectively unimodal, relying on a single type of information at a time-genomics alone, enviromics alone, physiology alone, or phenomics alo
papers.summary: This perspective argues that plant breeding has become multimodal in data collection (genomics, enviromics, phenomics) but still relies on unimodal prediction models, creating a critical gap. The authors advocate for integrating these diverse data types into unified prediction frameworks to fully leverage modern breeding data. They position multimodal genomic prediction as essential for improving accuracy and accelerating crop improvement.

papers.title: A knowledge graph and multi-agent reinforcement learning model for psychological risk identification and intervention in online learning.
papers.abstract: As the scale of online learning expanded, students tended to exhibit declining learning behaviors and accumulating task backlogs during self-directed study. These issues further led to reduced learning motivation and increased psychological pressure. To address this, this study constructed an innovative model that integrated a knowledge graph with multi-agent reinforcement learning. The model enabled learning state risk identification and personalized intervention. The study conducted a systematic evaluation using comprehensive learning behavior data from seven selected courses. The results in
papers.summary: This study developed a model combining knowledge graphs with multi-agent reinforcement learning to identify psychological risks in online learners and provide personalized interventions. Using data from seven courses, the model effectively detected declining motivation and task backlog, offering a new approach to supporting student mental health in digital learning environments.

papers.title: Integrative proteogenomic analyses identify plasma proteins that impact the risk of ischemic stroke.
papers.abstract: Understanding the proteins implicated in the pathogenesis of ischemic stroke is important for elucidating disease mechanisms and informing prevention strategies. In this study, we aim to identify plasma proteins with a potentially causal effect on risk of ischemic stroke by integrating the largest available genetic data sources for plasma proteins, ischemic stroke and its risk factors.
papers.summary: This study integrates large-scale genetic data to identify plasma proteins that may causally influence the risk of ischemic stroke, providing insights into disease mechanisms and potential prevention targets. By combining proteomic and genomic analyses, the authors pinpoint specific proteins whose levels could be modulated to reduce stroke risk.

papers.title: MetagenomicKG: a knowledge graph for metagenomic applications.
papers.abstract: The sheer volume and variety of genomic content within microbial communities makes metagenomics a field rich in biomedical knowledge. To traverse these complex communities and their vast unknowns, metagenomic studies often depend on distinct reference databases, such as the Genome Taxonomy Database (GTDB), the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG), and the Bacterial and Viral Bioinformatics Resource Center (BV-BRC), for various analytical purposes. These databases are crucial for the genetic and functional annotation of microbial communities. Nevertheless, the inconsistent nomenclatur
papers.summary: The paper introduces MetagenomicKG, a knowledge graph that integrates multiple reference databases (GTDB, KEGG, BV-BRC) to unify nomenclature and facilitate exploration of microbial communities. This resource aims to address the challenge of inconsistent naming across databases, making metagenomic analysis more coherent and efficient.

papers.title: Artificial intelligence optimization model for public safety in smart cities based on multi-source data collaboration in the internet of things.
papers.abstract: The prevalent cross-departmental data barriers and ineffective public safety risk management and control in smart city construction severely restrict governance efficiency. This study takes Hefei as an empirical case and innovatively proposes and validates a "technology-institution-data" ternary collaborative smart city public safety artificial intelligence optimization model. The core contribution lies in constructing a three-layer conceptual framework of "perception-decision-value", systematically integrating real-time perception driven by the Internet of Things (IoT), intelligent decision-m
papers.summary: This study proposes an AI optimization model for public safety in smart cities that integrates IoT-driven real-time perception with intelligent decision-making, using Hefei as a case study. The model aims to overcome cross-departmental data barriers and improve governance efficiency through a 'technology-institution-data' collaborative framework.

papers.title: Mechanistically Informed and Omics-Guided Essential Oil Applications for Food Preservation.
papers.abstract: The growing demand for safe, sustainable, and clean-label food preservation strategies has accelerated interest in plant-derived essential oils (EOs) as natural antimicrobial agents. These chemically complex mixtures exhibit broad-spectrum activity against foodborne pathogens and spoilage microorganisms through multitarget mechanisms, including membrane disruption, metabolic interference, oxidative stress induction, and modulation of quorum sensing and biofilm formation. However, their practical application remains constrained by compositional variability, physicochemical instability, matrix-d
papers.summary: This review explores how essential oils (EOs) can serve as natural antimicrobials for food preservation, focusing on their multi-target mechanisms like membrane disruption and biofilm inhibition. The authors discuss challenges such as compositional variability and instability, and propose that omics-guided approaches could help optimize EO applications for safer, clean-label food preservation.

papers.title: Targeting mir130b-IL-33-PD-L1 axis effectively inhibits esophageal squamous carcinoma progression.
papers.abstract: We used an integrated approach combining molecular biology techniques, animal experiments, and clinical samples to elucidate the mechanistic basis of interleukin-33 (IL-33)-induced programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) upregulation and its role in squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) progression. Multi-omics analyses and clinical datasets identified that increased expression of IL-33 and its receptor in tumor tissues correlated with advanced stage and poor prognosis. IL-33 expression was associated with enhanced migration, invasion, and proliferation of tumor cells, and these effects were partly reverse
papers.summary: This study identifies a new regulatory axis (mir130b-IL-33-PD-L1) that drives esophageal squamous cell carcinoma progression. Using multi-omics and clinical data, the authors show that IL-33 upregulates PD-L1, promoting tumor cell migration and invasion, and that targeting this axis could be a potential therapeutic strategy.

papers.title: Enhancing medical Q&A systems with multimodal knowledge graphs and dual-layer attention mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Medical intelligent question-answering (QA) systems have become important tools for improving the efficiency of healthcare services, and recent research has increasingly emphasized performance optimization and multimodal integration. However, existing systems still face several challenges in intent recognition, entity extraction, and multimodal knowledge fusion, particularly reduced accuracy in multi-label classification, heavy reliance on large-scale annotated data, and limited support for cross-modal retrieval. To address these issues, this study proposes a medical intelligent QA framework t
papers.summary: This paper presents a new framework for medical question-answering systems that uses multimodal knowledge graphs and dual-layer attention mechanisms to improve intent recognition, entity extraction, and cross-modal retrieval. The approach aims to overcome limitations in existing systems such as reduced accuracy in multi-label classification and heavy reliance on annotated data.

papers.title: Group cognitive space of online rumors in public health emergencies: a theoretical and empirical study.
papers.abstract: Internet rumors related to public health emergencies often trigger public panic and result in resource mismatches. During such crises, the public's cognition is not fixed but continuously undergoes a dynamic process from initial construction to reconstruction, eventually solidifying into a stable "group cognitive space." Investigating the structure of this space is crucial for understanding heterogeneous public behaviors and enhancing emergency governance.
papers.summary: This study explores how public cognition of online rumors during health emergencies evolves from initial formation to a stable 'group cognitive space,' which influences diverse public behaviors. By analyzing this dynamic cognitive structure, the research aims to improve emergency management and reduce panic caused by misinformation.

papers.title: Microbial Ecology at the Nexus of Food Safety and Biotechnology With Ecological Mechanisms, Risks, and Emerging Innovations.
papers.abstract: Food systems are complex microbial ecosystems in which microorganisms play dual and often contrasting roles as agents of foodborne contamination and as essential drivers of food production and biotechnological innovation. Microbial ecology provides an integrative framework for understanding how microbial interactions, environmental conditions, and human interventions shape food safety outcomes and technological processes. This narrative integrative review is aimed at synthesizing current literature on microbial ecology at the nexus of food safety and food biotechnology and at identifying key r
papers.summary: This review explores how microbial ecology connects food safety and biotechnology, highlighting the dual roles of microorganisms as both contaminants and beneficial agents in food production. It synthesizes current knowledge on microbial interactions and environmental factors that influence food safety and biotechnological processes.
